compilers.python: 3.10.12 (GIL enabled; no free-threaded build available on this machine)

machine.notes: Medians of repeated interactive runs on a shared multi-tenant host; treat as order-of-magnitude, not microarchitectural truth.

creation_cost_us.method: N=1000-5000 create+join (or fork+wait) pairs, CLOCK_MONOTONIC, warmup excluded, median of 2-3 runs

context_switch.method: parent and child pinned to the same core, 1-byte ping-pong over two pipes, 199k round trips; each round trip forces two context switches

data_race_lost_updates.method: volatile long counter++ from 4 threads, gcc -O2; volatile forces the load/add/store to memory each iteration but provides no atomicity

counter_throughput_Mops.note: sharded = per-thread alignas(64) counter summed at the end; the single-thread volatile increment sustains roughly one store-forwarded increment per cycle

false_sharing_Mops.method: each thread increments its OWN atomic<long> with relaxed ordering; packed = counters 8 bytes apart in one cache line, padded = alignas(64)

store_buffer_litmus.test: SB: thread A does X=1; r1=Y, thread B does Y=1; r2=X, both variables initially 0
store_buffer_litmus.method: threads pinned to distinct cores, released per trial by a coordinator via an acquire-loaded sequence number; relaxed compiles to plain mov store + mov load, seq_cst store compiles to an instruction that drains the store buffer (mfence or xchg)

spsc_queue_Mops.method: one producer, one consumer, acquire/release ring buffer vs std::queue under std::mutex with two condition variables; high variance across runs on this shared host

cpu_bound.task: sum of i*i for i in range(2_000_000), pure Python, 8 tasks

python_gil.free_threaded: unavailable: this machine has CPython 3.10 only; the free-threaded comparison requires a 3.13+ build configured with --disable-gil

contention_scaling_Mops.method: C++ aggregate increments/s, 2,000,000 iterations per thread, best of 3 runs; mutex = std::mutex + lock_guard, atomic = fetch_add(relaxed) on one word, sharded = per-thread alignas(64) atomic

lockfree_stack_Mops.method: pop-then-push pairs on a shared stack, best of 3 runs each; Treiber = CAS loop on head, mutex = std::stack behind std::mutex; nodes preallocated, no reclamation
lockfree_stack_Mops.note: multi-threaded numbers vary by 2x run to run on this shared host; the reliable finding is that lock-free is ~1.5x faster single-threaded and roughly a wash under contention, because both designs are bounded by one cache line

ms_queue_check.configuration: Michael-Scott lock-free MPMC queue, 4 producers x 200,000 enqueues, 4 consumers
ms_queue_check.result: exact match; no lost or duplicated items

deadlock_demo.broken: two accounts, two threads transferring in opposite directions, locks taken in argument order: hangs; killed by a 5 s timeout (exit 124)
deadlock_demo.fixed: std::scoped_lock over both mutexes: 400 transfers complete, balances conserved (a=1000, b=1000, sum=2000)

python_start_methods_s.method: multiprocessing.get_context(m); single Process(target=noop) start+join is the median of 5, Pool(4)+map(noop) is the best of 3 and includes interpreter or forkserver bring-up

python_data_transfer.note: shared_memory sends only a name; the worker maps the same pages and sums them in place

python_executors_s.note: ThreadPoolExecutor(8) needs 4 waves of 8; 32 concurrent waiters finish in one wave

tooling.tsan_detects: gcc 11.4 -fsanitize=thread reports the unsynchronized counter++ as 'data race', naming both accesses, both threads, and the global; requires ASLR off on this kernel (setarch -R)
